perturbation.original_text: Selling Parties hereby agree that the period of time in which this Section 1 is in effect shall be extended for a period equal to the duration of any breach of this Section 1(a) by Selling Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Parties hereby agree that the period of time in which this Section 1 is in effect shall not be extended regardless of any breach of this Section 1(a) by Selling Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly stating that the non-compete period will not be extended even in the event of a breach, the contract becomes less enforceable. Nevada law allows for the extension of non-compete agreements in cases of breach. This omission creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NRS 613.190 Restraint of trade: Unlawful contracts, combinations and conspiracies.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 613.190 outlines the conditions under which non-compete agreements can be enforced. By stating that the non-compete period will not be extended even if breached, the contract contradicts Nevada law that could allow for extensions to make the non-compete agreement effective and provide adequate remedy.

NRS 613.190 Corrupt influencing of employee unlawful. Every person who shall give, offer or promise, directly or indirectly, any compensation, gratuity or reward to any agent, employee or servant of any person or corporation, with intent to influence the action of the agent, employee or servant in relation to his or her principal's, employer's or master's business, shall be guilty of a gross misdemeanor. perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the non-compete period will not be extended regardless of a breach, which directly contradicts the Nevada law's potential allowance for extensions. The original text allows for the extension, whereas the changed text removes that possibility. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both confirm this contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 contains the line: 'NRS 613.195 Noncompetition covenants: Limitations; enforceability; revision by court; award to prevailing party.' This suggests that non-compete agreements and their enforceability can be revised by the court, implying flexibility in their terms. By removing the possibility of extension, the changed text limits this flexibility and contradicts the potential for court revision, as it makes no provision for extension even if a breach warrants it.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Parties recognize and acknowledge that the Company's trade secrets and all other confidential and proprietary information of a business, financial or other nature, including without limitation, proprietary information of the Company, as it exists from time to time (collectively, "Confidential Information"), are valuable and unique assets of the Company and therefore agree that, during the Restriction Period, except as otherwise required by Applicable Laws, or the rules of any exchange on which any securities of Zenith are or will be listed, they will not, and will use their best efforts to ensure that their directors, officers, employees, advisers, agents and consultants do not, disclose any Confidential Information concerning the Company and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates, to any person, firm, corporation, association or other entity, for any reason whatsoever, unless previously authorized in writing to do so by Meritage.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Parties recognize and acknowledge that the Company's confidential information (collectively, "Confidential Information"), are valuable assets of the Company and therefore agree that, during the Restriction Period, they will not disclose any Confidential Information concerning the Company and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates, to any person, firm, corporation, association or other entity, for any reason whatsoever, unless authorized in writing to do so by Meritage.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of 'trade secrets and all other proprietary information of a business, financial or other nature' reduces the scope of what is considered confidential information, making it more difficult for the Company to protect its sensitive data. This weakens protection, creating a legal risk. By removing the phrase 'except as otherwise required by Applicable Laws, or the rules of any exchange on which any securities of Zenith are or will be listed' the seller is now obligated to keep silent even if required to disclose information through legal means, creating a possible violation of law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets. While this agreement mentions confidential information, narrowing the definition and omitting the clause about legal disclosure requirements makes it more difficult to enforce protection, potentially conflicting with the intent of the DTSA.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits 'trade secrets and all other proprietary information of a business, financial or other nature', which weakens the protection of sensitive company data. Additionally, removing the clause 'except as otherwise required by Applicable Laws, or the rules of any exchange on which any securities of Zenith are or will be listed' creates an obligation to keep silent even when legally required to disclose information. This contradicts legal obligations. Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets, indicating a legal framework for safeguarding such information. The omission weakens the company's ability to utilize this legal framework, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement, shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws, and not the law of conflicts, of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and all questions relating to its interpretation, performance and enforcement, shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws, and not the law of conflicts, of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'validity', the contract omits a crucial aspect of legal governance. If a challenge arises regarding the fundamental validity of the agreement itself (e.g., capacity to contract, duress), the contract provides no guidance and could open the door to arguments that the law governing interpretation should not apply to validity. This raises a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104.1103 states that the Uniform Commercial Code should be used to interpret and validate contracts. Removing 'validity' specifically from the governance clause creates a contradiction, as the UCC would be used to determine validity even if not explicitly stated.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NRS 104.1103 Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote underlying purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. :: Chapter 104 Uniform Commercial Code — Original Articles :: TITLE 8 COMMERCIAL INSTRUMENTS AND TRANSACTIONS :: 2010 Nevada Revised Statutes :: Nevada Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Nevada Revised Statutes 2024 Nevada Rev. Stat. 2023 Nevada Rev. Stat. 2022 Nevada Rev. Stat. 2010 Nevada Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Nevada Code TITLE 8 COMMERCIAL INSTRUMENTS AND TRANSACTIONS Chapter 104 Uniform Commercial Code — Original Articles NRS 104.1103 Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote underlying purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. NRS 104.1103 Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote underlying purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. 1. The Uniform Commercial Code must be liberally construed and applied to promote its underlying purposes and policies, which are: (a) To simplify, clarify and modernize the law governing commercial transactions; (b) To permit the continued expansion of commercial practices through custom, usage and agreement of the parties; and (c) To make uniform the law among the various jurisdictions. 2. Unless displaced by the particular provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code, the principles of law and equity, including the law merchant and the law relative to capacity to contract, principal and agent, estoppel, fraud, misrepresentation, duress, coercion, mistake, bankruptcy, and other validating or invalidating cause supplement its provisions. (Added to NRS by 2005, 824) Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Nevada may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on this site or the information
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'validity' from the contract's governance clause creates a contradiction. NRS 104.1103(2) states that 'the principles of law and equity, including the law merchant and the law relative to capacity to contract, principal and agent, estoppel, fraud, misrepresentation, duress, coercion, mistake, bankruptcy, and other validating or invalidating cause supplement its provisions.' By removing 'validity', the contract potentially excludes this crucial aspect from being governed by Nevada law as intended, potentially leading to disputes over whether Nevada law should apply to the validity of the agreement itself, contradicting the comprehensive nature of NRS 104.1103(2).

perturbation.original_text: Zenith agrees to pay any and all reasonable costs and expenses, including attorneys' fees, incurred by the Company in enforcing this provision if it is determined that Selling Parties breached this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Zenith agrees to pay reasonable costs incurred by the Company in enforcing this provision if it is determined that Selling Parties breached this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'any and all' and 'including attorneys' fees', the contract becomes less specific regarding the costs Zenith is responsible for, creating ambiguity and reducing the Company's ability to fully recover its enforcement expenses. Nevada law allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in contract disputes under certain circumstances, but requires specificity in the contract to ensure full recovery.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 18.010 allows a court to award attorney’s fees to the prevailing party when authorized by statute or agreement. By removing 'any and all' and specifically excluding ‘attorneys' fees’, the contract restricts the Company's ability to recover full legal costs, potentially conflicting with the spirit and intent of NRS 18.010 allowing for reasonable attorney's fees in contract enforcement.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes 'any and all' and 'including attorneys' fees,' which contradicts the intent of NRS 18.010. The law allows for attorney's fees to the prevailing party when authorized by agreement. The original text clearly specifies the Company's entitlement to all reasonable costs, including attorney's fees. By omitting 'any and all' and explicitly excluding 'attorneys' fees' the changed text limits the Company's ability to recover full legal costs, conflicting with the underlying principle of NRS 18.010. Snippet 1 shows that NRS 18.005 defines 'costs,' which pertains to NRS 18.010, the attorney's fees law. The change significantly weakens the company's ability to recover costs. This falls under the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction.
